---
title: "Product and quotient spaces"
description: "Product spaces, quotient spaces, affine subsets and combinations, the quotient map, and the first isomorphism theorem — an augmented summary of Axler's LADR §3.E."
date: "2026-06-06"
date-modified: "2026-06-19"
categories: ["mathematics", "linear-algebra", "ladr"]
execute:
    echo: true
---

{{< include ../../_macros.qmd >}}

This is an augmented summary of Section 3.E of [Linear Algebra Done Right](https://linear.axler.net/index.html) by Sheldon Axler.

## Product of vector spaces
The **product space** $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ of vector spaces $V_1,\cdots,V_n$ (all over $\mb{F}$) is their Cartesian product. (This shouldn't be confused with the product of linear maps.)

If we define addition and scalar multiplication on $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ as element-wise operations, we can show that the product space is a vector space over $\mb{F}$.

#### Dimension of product
:::{#thm-dim-product}
Suppose that the $V_i$s are finite-dimensional. Then $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ is also finite-dimensional, with its dimension given by the sum of the individual dimensions of the $V_i$s.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Decompose the product space into a direct sum.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

The result follows from the fact that $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ is a direct sum of the product spaces

$$
\begin{gather}
V_1\times\{0\}\times\cdots\times\{0\}\\
\vdots\\
\{0\}\times\cdots\times\{0\}\times V_n,
\end{gather}
$$

where the dimension of the $i$th space is given by $\dim V_i$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Basis of $\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R})\times\mathbb{R}^2$</summary>

The product space $\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R})\times\mathbb{R}^2$ consists of vectors like $(x^2-3,(1,2))$. A basis is given by

$$(1,(0,0)),(x,(0,0)),(x^2,(0,0)),(0,(1,0)),(0,(0,1)),$$

and we see that its size is equal to $\dim\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R})+\dim\mathbb{R}^2=3+2$.

</details>

:::{#thm-product-finite-implies-components}
Conversely, if $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ is finite-dimensional, we can show that the component spaces are finite-dimensional.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
From a basis for the product space, we can "extract" bases for the individual spaces.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

If $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ is finite-dimensional, then there exist vectors

$$(v_1^1,\cdots,v_n^1),\cdots,(v_1^m,\cdots,v_n^m)$$

that form a basis of this product space. But this implies that the set $v_i^1,\cdots,v_i^m\in V_i$ spans $V_i$ for each $i$, so the $V_i$s are finite-dimensional.

</details>

#### Product to sum
Let $U_1,\cdots,U_n$ be subspaces of $V$, and consider the linear map 

$$\Gamma:U_1\times\cdots\times U_n\to U_1+\cdots+U_n$$

that is defined as

$$\Gamma(u_1,\cdots,u_n)=u_1+\cdots+u_n.$$

By definition, $\Gamma$ is surjective.

###### Conditions for directness
:::{#thm-direct-iff-gamma-injective}
In the setup above, $U_1+\cdots+U_n$ is a direct sum if and only if $\Gamma$ is injective (and thereby invertible).
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

$U_1+\cdots+U_n$ is a direct sum.

$\iff$ $u_1+\cdots+u_n=0$ if and only if $u_1=\cdots=u_n=0$.

$\iff$ $\ker\Gamma=\{0\}$.

$\iff$ $\Gamma$ is injective.

</details>

:::{#thm-gamma-injective-iff-dim-sum}
Furthermore, $\Gamma$ is injective if and only if

$$\dim(U_1+\cdots+U_n)=\dim U_1+\cdots+\dim U_n.$$

:::

:::{.informal}
We already know that the directness of the sum implies

$$\dim(U_1+\cdots+U_n)=\dim U_1+\cdots+\dim U_n.$$

So here, we are establishing that we can determine the directness from the dimensions.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Use the rank-nullity theorem on $\Gamma$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

We have seen that 

$$\dim(U_1\times\cdots\times U_n)=\dim U_1+\cdots+\dim U_n,$$

where we note that $U_1\times\cdots\times U_n$ is the domain of $\Gamma$. Recall that $\Gamma$ is surjective, so 

$$\im\ \Gamma=U_1+\cdots+U_n.$$

Then by the rank-nullity theorem, we obtain

$$\dim(U_1\times\cdots\times U_n)=\dim(U_1+\cdots+U_n)$$

if and only if $\dim\ker\Gamma=0$, which is equivalent to the condition that $\Gamma$ is injective.

</details>

#### Splitting linear map of product
:::{#thm-split-map-from-product}
The vector space $\mathcal{L}(V_1\times\cdots\times V_n,W)$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal{L}(V_1,W)\times\cdots\times\mathcal{L}(V_n,W)$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
For each $T\in\mathcal{L}(V_1\times\cdots\times V_n,W)$, define linear maps $T_1,\cdots,T_n$ where each $T_i$ tracks the (necessarily linear) contribution of the $i$th coordinate towards the output.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

For each $T:V_1\times\cdots\times V_n\to W$, we can associate a series of maps $T_i:V_i\to W$ defined as

$$T_iv=T(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)$$

where $v$ occurs in the $i$th argument of $T$. Because $T$ is linear, each $T_i$ is also linear. So consider the map 

$$M:\mathcal{L}(V_1\times\cdots\times V_n,W)\to\mathcal{L}(V_1,W)\times\cdots\times\mathcal{L}(V_n,W)$$

that maps $T$ to $T_1\times\cdots\times T_n$. Since

$$
\begin{aligned}
(S+T)(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)&=S(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)+T(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)\\
&=S_iv+T_iv,
\end{aligned}
$$

we have 

$$M(S+T)=(S_1+T_1)\times\cdots\times(S_n+T_n)$$

and so $M$ is additive. Similarly, we can show that $M$ is homogeneous.

For $M(T)$ to be $0\times\cdots\times0$, $T$ must map each $(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)$ to $0$. If so, the linearity of $T$ leads to

$$
T(v_1,\cdots,v_n)=T(v_1,0,\cdots,0)+\cdots+T(0,\cdots,0,v_n)=0+\cdots+0=0,
$$

which means that $T$ is the zero map. This implies $\ker M=\{0\}$, and so $M$ is injective.

$M$ is surjective because for any set of linear maps $T_i:V_i\to W$, the map

$$T:(v_1,\cdots,v_n)\mapsto T_1v_1+\cdots+T_nv_n$$

is a linear map from $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ to $W$. (The linearity of $T$ follows from those of the individual $T_i$s.)

Therefore, $M$ is an isomorphism.

</details>

#### Splitting linear map to product
:::{#thm-split-map-to-product}
The vector space $\mathcal{L}(V,W_1\times\cdots\times W_n)$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal{L}(V,W_1)\times\cdots\times\mathcal{L}(V,W_n)$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
For each $T\in\mathcal{L}(V,W_1\times\cdots\times W_n)$, define linear maps $T_1,\cdots,T_n$ where each $T_i$ tracks the (necessarily linear) contribution of the input towards the $i$th output coordinate.

We can show that $M$ is an isomorphism, by following the steps similar to the previous proof.
:::

#### Isomorphism of vector power
:::{#thm-vector-power-iso}
The vector space $V^n$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal{L}(\mb{F}^n,V)$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
For a given $(v_1,\cdots,v_n)\in V^n$, there is only one natural definition of a function $\phi:(x_1,\cdots,x_n)\mapsto v$ that we can make.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $M:V^n\to\mathcal{L}(\mb{F}^n,V)$ be the map that sends each $(v_1,\cdots,v_n)$ to the function

$$\phi(x_1,\cdots,x_n)=x_1v_1+\cdots+x_nv_n.$$

The linearity of $\phi$ can easily be ascertained. $M$ is additive because

$$
M((v_1,\cdots,v_n)+(u_1,\cdots,u_n))=M(v_1+u_1,\cdots,v_n+u_n),
$$

which is sent to the function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(x_1,\cdots,x_n)&=x_1(v_1+u_1)+\cdots+x_n(v_n+u_n)\\
&=(x_1v_1+\cdots+x_nv_n)+(x_1u_1+\cdots+x_nu_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

where the two bracketed terms correspond to $M(v_1,\cdots,v_n)$ and $M(u_1,\cdots,u_n)$ respectively. Similarly, we can find that $M$ is homogeneous, and so $M$ is a linear map.

In order to have $x_1v_1+\cdots+x_nv_n=0$ for all $x_1,\cdots,x_n$, we must have $v_1=\cdots=v_n=0$. It follows that $\ker M=\{0\}$, and so $M$ is injective.

By the linearity of $\phi$, we have

$$\phi(x_1,\cdots,x_n)=x_1\phi(1,0,\cdots,0)+\cdots+x_n\phi(0,\cdots,0,1)=x_1\phi(e_1)+\cdots+x_n\phi(e_n)$$

where $e_i$ is the $i$th standard basis.
If we set $v_i=\phi(e_i)$, we see that $M(v_1,\cdots,v_n)=\phi$, and so $M$ is surjective.

Therefore, $M$ is an isomorphism.

</details>

## Quotient space
Let $U$ be a subspace of $V$. Then the **quotient space** $V/U$ is a set of subsets of $V$, given by

$$V/U=\{v+U:v\in V\}.$$

Each subset $v+U$ is called an **affine subset** of $V$ that is **parallel** to $U$.

<details class="example">
<summary>Quotient space in $\mathbb{R}^2$</summary>

Suppose that $U\subset\mathbb{R}^2$ is the set of points that lies on the line of slope $2$ through the origin, i.e.

$$U=\{(x,2x):x\in\mathbb{R}\}.$$

Then every line of slope $2$ is parallel to $U$, and $\mathbb{R}^2/U$ has as vectors the lines with slope $2$.

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Subspace of $\mb{F}^\infty$ with finite number of non-zero entries</summary>

Consider the subset of $\mb{F}^\infty$ defined as

$$U=\{(x_1,x_2,\cdots):x_i\neq0\text{ for finitely many }i\}.$$

We can check that $U$ is a subspace. 

Two vectors of $\mb{F}^\infty$ are parallel with respect to $U$ if they disagree at a finite number of entries. So consider the sequence of vectors $v_1,v_2,\cdots$ where the $j$th entry of $v_i$ is equal to $1$ if and only if $j$ is a multiple of $2^i$, i.e.

$$v_1=(0,1,0,1,0,\cdots,),\ v_2=(0,0,0,1,0,\cdots),$$

and so on. We wish to show that $v_1+U,\cdots,v_n+U$ is linearly independent for all $n$, which means that if $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n$ has a finite number of non-zero entries, then $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$.

We will show this by induction. For $n=1$, the claim is trivially true. Now suppose that the claim is true for $n=k-1$. For contradiction, suppose that there exists $a_1,\cdots,a_k$ not all zero such that $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_kv_k$ has a finite number of non-zero entries. In particular, $a_k$ cannot be zero by the induction hypothesis.

By construction, the sequence $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_kv_k$ is periodic. So if it has a single non-zero entry, it must have an infinite number of them. As we assumed that $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_kv_k$ has a finite number of non-zero entries, it must be uniformly zero.

Therefore, we require 

$$a_1v_1+\cdots+a_kv_k=(0,0,\cdots)\implies a_1v_1+\cdots+a_{k-1}v_{k-1}=-a_kv_k,$$ 

where $a_k\neq0$. Now, $a_kv_k$ has period $2^k$ but $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_{k-1}v_{k-1}$ has period $2^{k-1}$. This means that every $(2i-1)\cdot2^{k-1}$th entry is equal to every $2i\cdot2^{k-1}$th entry in the latter, while they are different in the former. So the required equality cannot hold, and $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_kv_k$ has a finite number of non-zero entries only when $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$.

By the principle of induction, we have $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n\in U$ only when $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$, regardless of $n$. Therefore, $v_1+U,\cdots,v_n+U$ is linearly independent for all $n$, and so $\mb{F}^\infty/U$ is infinite-dimensional. 

</details>

#### Two affine subsets are equal or disjoint
:::{#thm-affine-equal-or-disjoint}
For any $v,w\in V$ and a subspace $U$ of $V$, the following three statements are equivalent to each other:

1. $v-w\in U$.
2. $(v+U)\cap (w+U)\neq\emptyset$.
3. $v+U=w+U$.

:::

:::{.informal}
The affine subsets of a given subspace form a partition of the entire vector space, where the corresponding equivalence relation is $v\sim w$ if and only if $v-w\in U$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $v-w\in U$, so that $v-w=u$ for some $u\in U$. Then we have

$$v+0=w+u,$$

which means that this vector is in both $v+U$ and $w+U$ (since $0\in U$). So we find that $(v+U)\cap (w+U)\neq\emptyset$.

Suppose that $(v+U)\cap (w+U)\neq\emptyset$. Then there exist $u_1,u_2\in U$ such that $v+u_1=w+u_2$, from which we have $v=w+(u_2-u_1)$.

For any $v+u\in v+U$, we have

$$v+u=w+(u_2-u_1)+u,$$

which implies that $v+u\in w+U$ and so $v+U\subseteq w+U$. An analogous reasoning shows that $w+U\subseteq v+U$, hence we find $v+U=w+U$.

Suppose that $v+U=w+U$. Then there exist $u_1,u_2$ such that $v+u_1=w+u_2$, which leads to

$$v-w=u_2-u_1\in U.$$

Therefore, we see that (1) implies (2), (2) implies (3), and (3) implies (1). So the three statements are equivalent.

</details>

#### Equality of parallel subsets implies equality of subspaces
:::{#thm-parallel-equal-subspaces}
For any $v,x\in V$ and subspaces $U,W\subseteq V$, if $v+U=x+W$, then we have $U=W$.
:::

:::{.informal}
If two affine subspaces overlap, they must be parallel to the same subspace. (This is obvious if we think of affine subsets as translated copies of the subspace.)
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Try to express $W$ as an affine subset of $U$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $v+U=x+W$. Then for any $w\in W$, there exists $u\in U$ such that

$$v+u=x+w\implies w=(v-x)+u,$$

so we have $w\in(v-x)+U$ and $W\subseteq(v-x)+U$. 

In the same manner, for any $u\in U$, there exists $w\in W$ such that $v+u=x+w$. So similarly, we have $(v-x)+U\subseteq W$, which means that $W=(v-x)+U$.

Since $W$ is a subspace, so must $(v-x)+U$ be. This is only possible if $v-x$ is in $U$ and thereby does not translate $U$. In other words, we have $U=W$.

</details>

#### Intersection of affine subsets
:::{#thm-affine-intersection}
If $A_1$ and $A_2$ are affine subsets of $V$, then $A_1\cap A_2$ is either empty or is another affine subset.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
In the absence of other candidate affine subsets, we can conjecture that

$$A_1\cap A_2=x+(V_1\cap V_2)$$

where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are the subspaces parallel to $A_1$ and $A_2$ respectively.

It is helpful to think about two planes in $\mathbb{R}^3$ intersecting in a line, which is a translated subspace. 
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let us write $A_1=u_1+V_1$ and $A_2=u_2+V_2$, and suppose that their intersection is non-empty. So let $x$ be a vector in $A_1\cap A_2$, with $v_1\in V_1$ and $v_2\in V_2$ satisfying

$$x=u_1+v_1=u_2+v_2.$$

We wish to show that 

$$A_1\cap A_2\subseteq x+(V_1\cap V_2),$$ 

so let $y$ be an arbitrary vector in $A_1\cap A_2$. If $v'_1\in V_1$ and $v'_2\in V_2$ are vectors such that

$$y=u_1+v'_1=u_2+v'_2,$$

then we have $y-x\in V_1\cap V_2$ because

$$
\begin{gather}
y-x=(u_1+v'_1)-(u_1+v_1)=v'_1-v_1\in V_1\\
\text{and}\quad y-x=(u_2+v'_2)-(u_2+v_2)=v'_2-v_2\in V_2. 
\end{gather}
$$

As $y=x+(y-x)$ for any $y$, this implies that $y\in x+(V_1\cap V_2)$.

Now suppose that $y\in x+(V_1\cap V_2)$. Then $y=x+v$ for some $v\in V_1\cap V_2$, which implies that $y\in A_1\cap A_2$.

Therefore, $A_1\cap A_2=x+(V_1\cap V_2)$, and so $A_1\cap A_2$ is either empty or is an affine subset.

</details>

#### Affine combination
Given vectors $v_1,\cdots,v_n$, its **affine combination** is a special type of linear combination

$$\lambda_1v_1+\cdots+\lambda_nv_n$$

where the coordinates $\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_n$ sum to 1.

###### Condition for affine subset
:::{#thm-affine-combination-condition}
A non-empty subset $A$ of $V$ is an affine subset if and only if every affine combination of a pair of vectors in $A$ is also in $A$. 

(Note that an affine combination of two vectors can be written as $\lambda v_1+(1-\lambda)v_2$ without restricting $\lambda$.)
:::


:::{.proof-idea}
The subspace parallel to $A$ can be found by taking an arbitrary vector $w$ of $A$ as its representative vector, and writing other vectors of $A$ in the form $w+u$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $A$ is an affine subset, with $A=v+U$ for some $v\in V$ and a subspace $U\subseteq V$. For any two $v+u_1,v+u_2\in A$ and $\lambda\in\mb{F}$, we have

$$\lambda(v+u_1)+(1-\lambda)(v+u_2)=v+(\lambda u_1+(1-\lambda) u_2)\in v+U$$

as required.

Conversely, suppose that a non-empty subset $A\subseteq V$ is such that

$$\lambda v_1+(1-\lambda)v_2\in A$$

for all $v_1,v_2\in A$ and $\lambda\in\mb{F}$. Let $w$ be a vector in $A$. Then for all $v\in A$, we have

$$\lambda v+(1-\lambda)w=w+\lambda(v-w)\in A.$$

From this, we may conjecture that

$$A=w+\{\lambda(v-w):\lambda\in\mb{F},v\in A\}=w+U,$$

but first we have to verify that $U$ is a subspace. We see that $U$ contains $0$ and is homogeneous by definition. For two vectors $\lambda_1(v_1-w),\lambda_2(v_2-w)\in U$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1(v_1-w)+\lambda_2(v_2-w)&=(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)\left(\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}v_1+\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}v_2-w\right)\\
&=(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)\left(\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}v_1+\left(1-\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}\right)v_2-w\right),
\end{aligned}
$$

and so $U$ is additive as well. Therefore, $U$ is a subspace.

Suppose that $x\in A$. Then $x-w\in U$ and so

$$x=w+(x-w)\in w+U,$$

which implies $A\subseteq w+U$. Now suppose that $x\in w+U$. Then there exists $\lambda\in\mb{F}$ and $v\in A$ such that

$$x=w+\lambda(v-w)=\lambda v+(1-\lambda)w.$$

Because $w\in A$ as well, this implies that $x\in A$. So we have $w+U\subseteq A$. 

Therefore, $A$ is equal to the affine subset $w+U$.

</details>

###### Affine hull
:::{#thm-affine-hull}
Given $v_1,\cdots,v_n\in V$, its **affine hull**

$$A=\{\lambda_1v_1+\cdots+\lambda_nv_n:\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_n\in\mb{F},\lambda_1+\cdots+\lambda_n=1\}$$

is the smallest affine subset that contains $v_1,\cdots,v_n$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
We can use the constraint equation to remove $\lambda_1$ from the defining relation, from which we can determine the subspace parallel to the hull.

For the "smallest" part, note that a general affine subset can be translated such that $v_1$ is sent to $0$, where it becomes the subspace parallel to the original subset.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Using the constraint on the $\lambda_i$s to eliminate $\lambda_1$, we see that $A$ consists of vectors

$$(1-\lambda_2-\cdots-\lambda_n)v_1+\lambda_2v_2+\cdots+\lambda_nv_n.$$

This shows that $A$ is the affine subset

$$
A=v_1+\{(-\lambda_2-\cdots-\lambda_n)v_1+\lambda_2v_2+\cdots+\lambda_nv_n:\lambda_2,\cdots,\lambda_n\in\mb{F}\}=v_1+U,
$$

where we may easily check that $U$ is a subspace.

Any affine subspace that contains $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ can be written as $v_1+W$ where $W$ is a subspace containing the vectors

$$v_2-v_1,v_3-v_1,\cdots,v_n-v_1.$$

The smallest subspace that contains these vectors is their span, which is just $U$. So $A$ is the smallest affine subset containing $v_1,\cdots,v_n$.

</details>

#### Defining addition and multiplication
:::{#thm-quotient-operations-well-defined}
Addition and scalar multiplication defined on $V/U$ as

$$
\begin{gather}
(v+U)+(w+U)=(v+w)+U\\
\lambda(v+U)=(\lambda v)+U
\end{gather}
$$

are well-defined.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

For the addition to be well-defined, we require

$$(v+w)+U=(v'+w')+U$$

for any $v,w,v',w'\in V$ such that $v'-v$ and $w'-w$ are in $U$. For such $v'$ and $w'$, we have

$$(v'+w')-(v+w)=(v'-v)+(w'-w)\in U,$$

and so the required equality holds.

Similarly, we can show that $\lambda v+U=\lambda v'+U$, and so the scalar multiplication is well-defined.

</details>

From here, we can show that $V/U$ forms a vector space over $\mb{F}$ under these operations. As $0+U=U$ is the additive identity, $v+U$ is a non-zero vector only when $v\notin U$.

###### Constructing basis of $V$ from quotient space
:::{#thm-basis-from-quotient}
If $v_1+U,\cdots,v_n+U$ is a basis of $V/U$ and $u_1,\cdots,u_m$ is a basis of $U$, then 

$$v_1,\cdots,v_n,u_1,\cdots,u_m$$

is a basis of $V$.
:::

:::{.informal}
We can obtain a basis of $V$ by a "divide and conquer approach" with $V/U$ and $U$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Show that the set is linearly independent.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Since $\dim V/U=\dim V-\dim U$ (see later), we see that the set has the right size to be a basis of $V$.

Suppose that 

$$a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n+b_1u_1+\cdots+b_mu_m=0.$$

Then $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n\in U$, which implies that

$$(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)+U=U$$

and so $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$. Consequently, the linear independence of $u_1,\cdots,u_m$ implies that $b_1=\cdots=b_m=0$. 

So $v_1,\cdots,v_n,u_1,\cdots,u_m$ is a set of linearly independent vectors with the right size, and is therefore a basis.

</details>

###### Splitting lemma for vector space
:::{#thm-splitting-lemma}
If $V/U$ is finite-dimensional, then $V$ is isomorphic to $U\times V/U$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
If we choose a basis of $V/U$, then we obtain a "canonical representative vector" for each equivalence class. Every vector in $V$ can then be decomposed into its canonical representative and a "displacement" from this representative.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $v_1+U,\cdots,v_n+U$ be a basis of $V/U$. Then for every $v\in V$, there exist unique $a_1,\cdots,a_n$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
v+U&=a_1(v_1+U)+\cdots+a_n(v_n+U)\\
&=(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)+U.
\end{aligned}
$$

Then we have $v-(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)\in U$, so we can define a map $M:V\to U\times V/U$ as

$$Mv=(v-(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n),(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)+U),$$

which is well-defined by the uniqueness of the $a_i$s.

If the RHS is $(0,U)$, then we must have $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$ by the linear independence of the $v_i+U$s. Subsequently, we obtain

$$0=v-(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)=v-0=v.$$

So $\ker M=\{0\}$ and $M$ is injective. 

Meanwhile, $M$ is surjective because $M(u+w)=(u,w+U)$ for an arbitrary $(u,w+U)\in U\times V/U$.

Therefore, $M$ is invertible and $V$ is isomorphic to $U\times V/U$.

</details>

###### Finding direct sum from quotient space
:::{#thm-direct-sum-from-quotient}
Suppose that $V/U$ is finite-dimensional. Then there is a subspace $W\subseteq V$ with $\dim W=\dim (V/U)$ such that $V=U\oplus W$.
:::

:::{.informal}
If quotienting $V$ by its subspace $U$ leads to a finite-dimensional space, then $U$ has a "direct sum buddy" with a dimension equal to $V/U$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
The representative vectors in a basis of $V/U$ is a set of $n$ vectors.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $v_1+U,\cdots,v_n+U$ be a basis of $V/U$, where $n=\dim(V/U)$. We will show that the span of $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is the subspace $W$ that we are seeking.

If $a_1,\cdots,a_n$ are such that

$$a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n=0,$$

then we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
U&=(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)+U\\
&=a_1(v_1+U)+\cdots+a_n(v_n+U).
\end{aligned}
$$

By the linear independence of the $v_i+U$s, this implies that $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$. So $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is linearly independent, and $W=\text{span}(v_1,\cdots,v_n)$ is a subspace of $V$ with the required dimension. 

For an arbitrary $v\in V$, there exist coordinates $b_i$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
v+U&=b_1(v_1+U)+\cdots+b_n(v_n+U)\\
&=(b_1v_1+\cdots+b_nv_n)+U,
\end{aligned}
$$

which means that 

$$v-(b_1v_1+\cdots+b_nv_n)\in U.$$

As $b_1v_1+\cdots+b_nv_n\in W$, this implies that $V=U+W$.

Now suppose that

$$c_1v_1+\cdots+c_nv_n\in W$$

is also in $U$. This means that

$$U=(c_1v_1+\cdots+c_nv_n)+U=c_1(v_1+U)+\cdots+c_n(v_n+U),$$

which leads to $c_1=\cdots=c_n=0$ by the linear independence of the $v_i+U$s. This implies that $U\cap W=\{0\}$.

Therefore, we have $V=U\oplus W$.

</details>

## Quotient map
The **quotient map** $\pi:V\to V/U$ is defined by

$$\pi(v)=v+U.$$

It is a linear map because

$$
\begin{gather}
\pi(v+w)=(v+w)+U=(v+U)+(w+U)=\pi(v)+\pi(w)\\
\pi(\lambda v)=\lambda v+U=\lambda(v+U)=\lambda\pi(v).
\end{gather}
$$

#### Factorisation into quotient map
:::{#thm-factorisation-quotient}
Suppose that $U$ is a subspace of $V$ and $T:V\to W$. Let $\pi$ be the quotient map from $V$ to $V/U$.

Then $T$ can be factorised as $T=S\circ \pi$ for some $S:V/U\to W$ if and only if $U\subseteq\ker T$.
:::

:::{.informal}
We can "factor out" a quotient map from a linear map only if the kernel of the former is contained in that of the latter. 
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Define $S$ as $T=S\circ\pi$ and show that it is well-defined.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose there exists such $S$ with $T=S\circ\pi$. Then for all $u\in U$, we find

$$Tu=S(\pi(u))=S(u+U)=S(0+U)=0,$$

which implies that $U\subseteq \ker T$.

Conversely, suppose that $U\subseteq\ker T$. We will define $S:V/U\to W$ as

$$S(v+U)=Tv.$$

If $v_1+U=v_2+U$, then we have $v_1-v_2\in\ker T$ and so $Tv_1=Tv_2$. Therefore, $S$ is well-defined, and $T=S\circ\pi$ by definition.

</details>

###### Pullback
:::{#thm-pullback}
Given $S:V/U\to W$, its **pullback** $\Gamma:\mathcal{L}(V/U,W)\to\mathcal{L}(V,W)$ by the quotient map $\pi:V\to V/U$ is the map

$$\Gamma(S)=S\circ\pi.$$

This is an injective linear map whose image is given by the annihilator of $U$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

It is easy to check that $\Gamma$ is linear, by the properties of the linear map product.

Suppose that $\Gamma(S)=\Gamma(S')$. This implies

$$
\begin{aligned}
&S(\pi(v))=S'(\pi(v))\text{ for all }v\in V\\
\implies&S(v+U)=S'(v+U)\text{ for all }v+U\in V/U
\end{aligned}
$$

and so $S=S'$. Therefore, $\Gamma$ is injective.

Let $T\in\im\ \Gamma$. Then there exists $S:V/U\to W$ such that $\Gamma(S)=T$. For any $u\in U$, we find

$$
Tu=S(\pi(u))=S(0+U)=0,
$$

and so $T$ is an annihilator of $U$. Now suppose that $T$ is an annihilator of $U$. Then $U\subseteq\ker T$, and we have seen that this implies the existence of $S$ such that $\Gamma(S)=T$. Therefore, we have $\im\ \Gamma=U^0$.

</details>

###### Dimension of quotient space
:::{#thm-dim-quotient}
Since the kernel of $\pi$ is $U$ and $\pi$ is surjective, the rank-nullity theorem gives us

$$\dim V/U=\dim\im\ \pi=\dim V-\dim\ker\pi=\dim V-\dim U.$$
:::

## First isomorphism theorem
:::{#thm-first-isomorphism}
Given a linear map $T:V\to W$, let us define $\tilde{T}:V/\ker T\to W$ as

$$\tilde{T}(v+\ker T)=Tv.$$

Then $\tilde{T}$ is an isomorphism between $V/\ker T$ and $\im\ T$.
:::

:::{.informal}
Any linear map produces an isomorphism between the domain quotiented by the map's kernel and the image. 
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
We first show that $\tilde{T}$ is a well-defined linear map, then prove that it is injective and surjective (when we take the codomain to be $\im\ T$).
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

If $v+\ker T=u+\ker T$, then $v-u\in\ker T$. This implies that $Tv=Tu$, so $\tilde{T}$ is well-defined.

The linearlity of $\tilde{T}$ follows from our definitions of addition and multiplication on $V/U$.
Suppose that $\tilde{T}(v+\ker T)=\tilde{T}(w+\ker T)$. This implies $Tv=Tw$, and so $v-w\in\ker T$. Therefore, $v+\ker T=w+\ker T$ and $\tilde{T}$ is injective.

By the definition of $\tilde{T}$, we see that the images of $\tilde{T}$ and $T$ coincide. So $\tilde{T}$ is surjective on $\im\ T$. 

Hence, $V/\ker T$ is isomorphic to $\im\ T$. 

</details>

This is called the **first isomorphism theorem**.

#### Subspace with quotient space of dimension $1$
:::{#thm-}
Suppose that $U$ is a subspace of $V$. Then $\dim V/U=1$ if and only if $U$ is the kernel of a linear functional $\phi\in\mathcal{L}(V,\mb{F})$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $\dim V/U=1$, and fix some arbitrary $v_0\in V-U$. Then any affine subset parallel to $U$ can be written as $\lambda(v_0+U)=\lambda v_0+U$, and any $v\in V$ can be expressed as $v=\lambda_vv_0+u$ for some $\lambda_v\in\mb{F}$ and $u\in U$. 

Let us define $\phi:V\to\mb{F}$ as $\phi(v)=\lambda_v$. This is well-defined because every $v$ belongs to exactly one affine subset $\lambda v_0+U$. It is linear as

$$
\begin{gather}
v+w=(\lambda_vv_0+u)+(\lambda_wv_0+u')=(\lambda_v+\lambda_w)v_0+(u+u')\\
av=a(\lambda_vv_0+u)=(a\lambda_v)v_0+au
\end{gather}
$$

and so $\phi(v+w)=\phi(v)+\phi(w)$ and $\phi(av)=a\phi(v)$.

The kernel of $\phi$ consists of vectors that lies in $U$, as required.

Conversely, suppose that there is a non-zero $\phi\in\mathcal{L}(V,\mb{F})$, and let us write $U=\ker\phi$. Since $\phi$ is non-zero, we have

$$\dim\im\ \phi=\dim\mb{F}=1.$$

By the first isomorphism theorem, we have

$$\dim V/U=\dim(V/\ker\phi)=\dim\im\ \phi=1.$$

</details>
